title: Scotch Pie
subtitle: A hot water crust double pie filled with seasoned minced mutton or lamb, distinctive for its collar lid
excerpt: The Scotch pie is a Scottish institution: a round double-crust hot water pastry pie filled with highly seasoned mutton mince, with a distinctive collar that sits proud of the lid. They are eaten hot at football grounds, at New Year, and as a daily lunch staple across Scotland.
sourceNotes: Traditional Scottish baking. F. Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929): mutton pie method. Traditional Scottish baker's practice, Glasgow and Edinburgh variants.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked pies for up to 2 months. Reheat from frozen at 180°C for 20-25 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: The recipe doubles easily. Make 8 pies and freeze half for fast lunches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pies can be baked and stored refrigerated for 2 days, then reheated.

glossaryTerms.definition: The raised rim on a Scotch pie: the pastry walls are deliberately made taller than the filling, leaving a space above the lid that can be filled with baked beans, mashed potato, or gravy when serving, or simply acts as a grip when eating by hand.

Meat from a sheep over two years old, with a stronger and more complex flavour than lamb. Traditional Scotch pies are made with mutton; modern versions often use lamb mince. Mutton is available from specialist butchers and Middle Eastern meat shops.

content.text: The Scotch pie has a defining structural feature: the

. The hot water crust walls are formed about 3 cm taller than the filling level, with the disc lid sitting inside the walls rather than on top. This creates the distinctive rim above the lid that a Scotch pie fan will recognise immediately.

mince, heavily seasoned with nutmeg, mace, and pepper. Lamb mince is the standard substitute; use whichever you can find. The pies are eaten hot, often with a layer of baked beans tipped into the collar.

items.prepNote: or lamb stock; to moisten the filling

content.text: with 125 ml of water, bring just to the boil, and pour into the flour. Stir quickly and turn onto a floured board. Knead for 1 minute until smooth. Keep warm by covering with a bowl while working.

Divide the dough into 4 pieces. Reserve a quarter of each piece for the lid. Use the remaining three quarters to mould a cylinder 8 cm in diameter and 6 cm tall, using the base of a glass jar as a former if needed. The walls should be about 4 mm thick. Place on a lined baking tray. Mix the
. Pack tightly into each case to 1 cm below the rim. Roll the reserved dough into a disc and set it inside the walls as a lid. Brush with beaten egg.
Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Bake for 40-45 minutes until golden all over. A probe pushed through the lid should read 75°C in the filling centre.

rows.symptom: Pastry walls are uneven or collapsing
rows.cause: Pastry cooled too much before moulding, becoming brittle
rows.fix: Work quickly while the pastry is still warm. Keep it covered between pieces.

rows.symptom: Filling is very dense and dry
rows.cause: Insufficient fat in the mince, or no liquid added
rows.fix: Use mutton or lamb mince with at least 15% fat. Add the stock to the filling and mix well.

rows.cause: Pastry was too thick and baked unevenly
rows.fix: Aim for a consistent 4 mm wall. A small crack at the collar is cosmetic only.

rows.symptom: Pies slide on the baking tray
rows.cause: Fat from the mince leaks through the base during baking
rows.fix: Use baking paper to line the tray; this is normal for a hot-water-crust pie with a fat-rich filling.

content.text: The Scotch pie is as Scottish as haggis and as quotidian as a sandwich. It is the pie eaten at Hampden Park, at Hogmanay supper tables, and from paper bags outside baker's shops in Glasgow and Edinburgh on a cold morning. To eat it properly is to eat it hot, with the collar filled with something that contrasts: beans, mash, or just the cooking juices.
